Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity:
Nadejda Odoni, a 76-year-old female, is being admitted to home health care following a recent hospital discharge. She was admitted to the hospital on December 28, 2025, due to a mechanical
fall while on a cruise in Hawaii, leading to a closed humeral head fracture and subsequent hospitalization, which lasted until January 4, 2026. The primary diagnosis for her admission is an
unspecified displaced fracture of the surgical neck of the left humerus, with secondary diagnoses including osteoporosis with current pathological fractures, interstitial pulmonary disease,
hypothyroidism, muscle weakness, glaucoma, and essential hypertension.
Upon discharge, Ms. Odoni presents with significant functional limitations, including pain and decreased range of motion in her left shoulder, generalized muscle weakness, and a history of falls,
which necessitate skilled nursing and rehabilitation services. These limitations hinder her ability to perform activities of daily living (ADLs) independently, such as bathing, dressing, and mobility
within her home environment. She requires assistance with lower and upper body dressing and functional mobility due to the ongoing effects of her shoulder fracture and surgery, which involved
a reverse shoulder arthroplasty on January 26, 2026.
The skilled care required for Ms. Odoni includes skilled nursing for medication management, wound care, pain management, and monitoring her chronic conditions, along with physical therapy
to improve her strength, range of motion, and overall functional capacity. The medical necessity for these services is substantiated by her recent discharge from a skilled nursing facility, ongoing
rehabilitation needs, and the complexity of her medical conditions, which collectively indicate a need for comprehensive home health services to facilitate her recovery and transition back to
independence. It is essential for her safety and health that she receives coordinated care to manage her rehabilitation effectively and address her multiple medical issues in the home setting.
Criteria One (One must be met): Describe what assistive devices the patient is dependent on (if any) and specifically why the patient condition warrants the special transportation or the assistance
of another person or describe the condition that makes leaving home medically contraindicated.

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only no surgical or
procedure codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom
control for each condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter
the ICD-10-CM code for the condition described in Column 1 no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and
sequencing requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W, X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be
reported in M1023 (Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
ODONI, NADEJDA | 107 I DOB: 09/30/1949 I N: 03/06/2026
Column 2 the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control
for the condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W, X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control
appropriate for each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time 1 Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with
difficulty, affecting daily functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3- Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4 - Symptoms poorly
controlled; history of re-hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are
separate items and sequencing may not coincide.

Describe the patient's mental status. Include general appearance, behaviors, emotional responses, mental functioning, overall social interaction as well as both clinical objective observations and
subjective descriptions reported during this visit.
Patient was alert and oriented x3-4 at the time of assessment. Patient presented with mild anxiety and repeatedly expressed concern that her home environment was not clean;
however, the home was observed to be clean, organized, and free of visible safety hazards. The patient also verbalized ongoing concerns regarding the recently installed grab bars in
the bathroom. Patient reported dissatisfaction with the amount of money spent on the equipment and feels the placement of the grab bars is inaccurate and may pose a safety risk

(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed): (Mark all that apply.
1. Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2. Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3. Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4. Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5. Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7. None of the above behaviors demonstrated

0. Adequate - sees fine detail, such as regular print in newspapers/books

4. Severely impaired - no vision or sees only light, colors or shapes; eyes do not appear to follow objects

(M1311) Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact
or open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not
obscure the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often
includes undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to non removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable
pressure ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury

(M1033) Risk for Hospitalization: Which of the following signs or symptoms characterize this patient as at risk for hospitalization? (Mark all that apply.)
1. History of falls (2 or more falls - or any fall with an injury - in the past 12 months)
2. Unintentional weight loss of a total of 10 pounds or more in the past 12 months
3. Multiple hospitalizations (2 or more) in the past 6 months
4. Multiple emergency department visits (2 or more) in the past 6 months
5. Decline in mental, emotional, or behavioral status in the past 3 months
6. Reported or observed history of difficulty complying with any medical instructions (for example, medications, diet, exercise) in the past 3 months

1. Dependent A helper completed the activities for the patient.
A. Self Care: Code the patient's need for assistance with bathing, dressing, using the toilet, or
eating prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injur
B. Indoor Mobility (Ambulation): Code the patient's need for assistance with walking from room
to room (with or without a device such as cane, crutch or walker) prior to the current illness,
C. Stairs: Code the patient's need for assistance with internal or external stairs (with or without a
device such as cane, crutch, or walker) prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
D. Functional Cognition: Code the patient's need for assistance with planning regular tasks, such

Indicate devices and aids used by the patient prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury (Check all that apply)
Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance - If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
04. Supervision or touching assistance - Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided
03. Partial/moderate assistance Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)
88. Not attempted due to medical conditions or safety concerns
Current ability to tend safely to personal hygiene needs (specifically: washing face and hands, hair care, shaving or make up, teeth or denture care, or fingernail care)
0. Able to groom self unaided, with or without the use of assistive devices or adapted methods.
1. Grooming utensils must be placed within reach before able to complete grooming activities.
2. Someone must assist the patient to groom self.
3. Patient depends entirely upon someone else for grooming needs
Current Ability to Dress Upper Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, pullovers, front-opening shirts and blouses, managing zippers, buttons, and snaps:
0. Able to get clothes out of closets and drawers, put them on and remove them from the upper body without assistance.
1. Able to dress upper body without assistance if clothing is laid out or handed to the patient.
2. Someone must help the patient put on upper body clothing.
3. Patient depends entirely upon another person to dress the upper body.
Current Ability to Dress Lower Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, slacks, socks or nylons, shoes:
0. Able to obtain, put on, and remove clothing and shoes without assistance.
1. Able to dress lower body without assistance if clothing and shoes are laid out or handed to the patient.
2. Someone must help the patient put on undergarments, slacks, socks or nylons, and shoes.
3. Patient depends entirely upon another person to dress lower body.
Current ability to wash entire body safely. Excludes grooming (washing face, washing hands, and shampooing hair).
0. Able to bathe self in shower or tub independently, including getting in and out of tub/shower.
1. With the use of devices, is able to bathe self in shower or tub independently, including getting in and out of the tub/shower.
2. Able to bathe in shower or tub with the intermittent assistance of another person: (a) for intermittent supervision or encouragement or reminders, OR (b) to get in and out of
the shower or tub, OR (c) for washing difficult to reach areas.
3. Able to participate in bathing self in shower or tub, but requires presence of another person throughout the bath for assistance or supervision.
4. Unable to use the shower or tub, but able to bathe self independently with or without the use of devices at the sink, in chair, or on commode.
5. Unable to use the shower or tub, but able to participate in bathing self in bed, at the sink, in bedside chair, or on commode, with the assistance or supervision of another
6. Unable to participate effectively in bathing and is bathed totally by another person.
Current ability to get to and from the toilet or bedside commode safely and transfer on and off toilet/commode
0. Able to get to and from the toilet and transfer independently with or without a device
1. When reminded, assisted, or supervised by another person, able to get to and from the toilet and transfer.
2. Unable to get to and from the toilet but is able to use a bedside commode (with or without assistance).
3. Unable to get to and from the toilet or bedside commode but is able to use a bedpan/urinal independently

PLAN OF CARE AND SKILLED INTERVENTIONS
Orders and Treatment (Specify Frequency, Duration and Purpose of Visits): 484.60 (2.iv)
ADMIT PATIENT TO STAR HOME HEALTH CARE INC.
ALL DIAGNOSES AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN.
The patient is homebound that requires a skilled nursing for medication management, wound care, pain management, and monitoring her chronic conditions, along with physical therapy to
improve her strength, range of motion, and overall functional capacity. The medical necessity for these services is substantiated by her recent discharge from a skilled nursing facility, ongoing
rehabilitation needs, and the complexity of her medical conditions, which collectively indicate a need for comprehensive home health services to facilitate her recovery and transition back to
independence. It is essential for her safety and health that she receives coordinated care to manage her rehabilitation effectively and address her multiple medical issues in the home setting.
HH SN Frequency: 1 time per week for 1 week, For evaluation only, for a skillful observation and assessment on integumentary, musculoskeletal, respiratory, gastrointestinal, genitourinary,
endocrine, cardiovascular, and neurological status, medication reconciliation, pain assessment, infection control measures, diet, home safety, and disease process education.
HH PT Frequency: For evaluation and treatment to continue for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety, and fall prevention techniques training
Surgical wound at left shoulder: Fully granulating. SN instructed patient and caregiver to keep surgical incision clean, dry and intact until the next MD appointment.
The patient will verbalize knowledge regarding the disease process and management, including diet/medication management, safety, and infection control measures.
Patient will achieve maximum functional potential
Patient COVID-19 screening completed prior to visit. No report of symptoms or known COVID-19 exposure per patient/family. The patient was provided with a mask for use during the HH visit. The
patient agreed to use a mask during treatment. The clinician performed hand hygiene, donned a mask, face shield, and gloves, and followed infection control and bag technique procedures during
the visit. No COVID-related concerns during the time of the visit. Disinfection of equipment followed including dry time. Proper disposal and doffing were done upon completion of the visit

Explained Home Health service to patient/family/caregiver(s). Explained consent to care, release of liability, and patient rights & responsibilities. Consent to care, a liability release, and consent to
photograph were signed by the patient/responsible party. A comprehensive assessment of all body systems with OASIS elements evaluation was initiated. Reconciled facility discharges medications
against routine home prescriptions without issue. Discussed advance directives, privacy policy, COVID-19 prevention, 911 protocols, basic home safety, and fall prevention principles with
patient/family/caregiver(s). Discussed the plan of care draft with the patient and explained what to expect in the next visits and the remainder of the care episode. Also explained that the initial
discussion on the management of other disease processes will be done on the next visit.
During the home assessment, the patient walked with the clinician throughout the home and demonstrated the location and use of grab bars installed in both bathrooms. In the hallway bathroom, the
grab bar appears to be placed in a position that requires the patient to lean and pull with the upper extremity in order to utilize it. This movement places increased strain on the shoulder and may
contribute to increased pain. In the patient's primary bathroom, a shower chair is present; however, it appeared somewhat unsteady and may require further evaluation for safety.
Patient verbalized eagerness to begin physical therapy services to improve range of motion and strength of the left humerus following recent surgery. Patient states she understands her current
physical limitations related to the surgery and expressed a strong desire to regain her prior level of function.
Patient reports independence with medication management and states she takes medications as prescribed using an established system that works well for her. Vital signs obtained during the visit
were within normal limits. Surgical wound was assessed and appears to be healing appropriately with no visible signs of complications at this time. Patient reports she has a scheduled six-week
Although infection is not currently suspected, patient was educated on the signs and symptoms of possible infection, including redness, swelling, warmth, drainage, fever, and increased pain at the
surgical site. Patient verbalized understanding of the education provided and was instructed to report any concerning symptoms to her physician or home health provider. Patient remains motivated
and engaged in her care and is looking forward to participating in therapy to improve mobility, strength, and functional independence.
SN completed home assessment including vital signs, pain level, functional mobility, surgical site condition, and adherence to orthopedic precautions. Non-pitting edema noted to left upper extremity
with mild shoulder girdle weakness. SN reviewed physician precautions including non-weight bearing to the left shoulder, avoidance of excessive overhead movements, and restriction from driving.

Instruction provided on protecting the healing humeral fracture by keeping the affected arm supported when sitting or ambulating and avoiding lifting or pushing with the left arm. Patient was guided in
positioning techniques such as supporting the arm with pillows while resting and maintaining proper alignment when lying or sitting to decrease strain on the shoulder joint. Education provided on
recognizing changes that could indicate complications such as increased swelling, redness, drainage, fever, or sudden increase in pain. Teaching also included importance of adequate nutrition with
calcium and vitamin D intake, safe medication use including prescribed opioid analgesics, and use a protective cloth barrier to decrease discomfort and swelling. Patient verbalized understanding of
fracture precautions and pain control strategies.

SN assessed patient's gait, transfer ability, home layout, and environmental hazards due to history of ground-level fall and recent surgery. Patient resides in a one-level home with three steps to enter
the house and two steps leading to the living room. Patient demonstrated cautious ambulation with guarding of the left arm and reported generalized weakness. SN evaluated entryways, flooring
surfaces, lighting, and stair access. Instruction provided on maintaining clear walkways, removing loose or cluttered items, securing rugs or using non-skid backing, and ensuring adequate lighting
especially near steps and bathroom areas. Patient was instructed to wear socks with traction or non-skid footwear while ambulating in the home and to use handrails when navigating steps.
Guidance provided on pacing movements, rising slowly from sitting or lying positions to prevent dizziness, and keeping frequently used items within easy reach to avoid excessive reaching or
overhead movement. Patient was also advised to request assistance for tasks that require carrying objects or reaching above shoulder level due to non-weight bearing restriction. Education provided
on keeping a phone nearby in case of emergency and using stable furniture for support if needed. Patient verbalized understanding of fall-prevention strategies.
Patient verbalized 80% of understanding on the disease process taught during this visit. Patient unable to teach back complete understanding of signs and symptoms of surgical wound infection, pain
management, fall prevention measures and proper breathing exercises. Patient also redemonstrated 70% of home safety and fall prevention measures with the use of assistive device as per patient
still demonstrates weakness, poor endurance and ambulation
Start of care, Plan of care, visit frequencies, and update of patient health condition.
Patient's admission to Aplus HH, updated health condition, and visit frequencies.
Will continue to assess and evaluate the level of pain, patient understanding of the disease process, functional status, knowledge of self-care management, and overall patient health condition and
reinforce teachings on the next visit.
